title: Victoria sponge
subtitle: The classic British sponge, sandwiched with jam and cream
excerpt: The British standard: two even sponge layers made by the all-in-one method, sandwiched with strawberry jam and whipped cream, finished with a dusting of icing sugar. Makes one 20 cm cake.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: Victoria sponge documented as a sandwich cake made for afternoon tea, associated with the domestic habits of Queen Victoria. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the Victoria sandwich is recorded as the standard British household sponge. The all-in-one method with self-raising flour is the post-war British form, distinct from the Victorian creamed method, though the result is indistinguishable in a well-made cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled sponge layers. Wrap individually and freeze for up to 2 months. Fill from thawed.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The unfilled sponge layers can be baked a day ahead and wrapped in cling film. Fill just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake mixing technique where all the ingredients are beaten together at once rather than in the traditional creaming sequence of butter first, then sugar, then eggs, then flour. It produces a tender, even crumb because the fat coats the flour proteins before liquid is added, limiting gluten development. It requires a soft, room-temperature butter and self-raising flour.

content.text: The Victoria sponge is a measure of the oven. If both layers are level, well-risen, and the same height, the oven is doing its job. If one is taller, the tins are not in the same position — move them to the same shelf, or rotate halfway through the bake.
Equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs, and flour is the rule. Weigh the eggs in their shells first and use the same weight of each other ingredient.
Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line two 20 cm round tins. Using the

, beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{eggs}}, {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{whole-milk}} together in a large bowl until smooth and just combined, about 2 minutes with a hand mixer or 3–4 minutes by hand. Divide evenly between the tins and level the surface.

Bake for 23–25 minutes, until the sponges are well-risen, golden, and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The sponges will also have pulled away slightly from the sides of the tins. Cool in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a rack and cool completely.

Spread the {{jam}} over the flat side of one sponge. Spoon the lightly whipped {{double-cream}} on top and spread to the edges. Place the second sponge on top, flat side down. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} just before serving.
